column1: Skeleton/Structure - Check the box for each file that is included in the folder. Must be in the correct subdirectory.
column2: Code Style - check the applicable boxes
column3:
Reproducibility of report. You don't have to actually run anything, but is it clear to you based on what is in the "code" folder how all the figures/results in the report were produced? Would it be easy to reproduce them? If not, discuss why.

(Since I'm not asking you to run anything, I haven't provided any additional items students may have put in their "data" folders, but if e.g. the code refers to '../data/some-input-file.csv', pls assume it exists)
column4: How easy would it be for you to use their code to clean the data the same way they did? Is there an easy-to-use function in a separate file?
column5: Readability of report (Was the narrative clear and easy to read? Or did you find it hard to follow? Any grammar mistakes?)
column6: Discuss the data cleaning (Was the cleaning described in detail? Were there any inconsistencies in the data that were missed?)
column7: Relevance of figures - excluding findings (were the figures relevant and discussed in the report?)
column11: Finding 3 Quality (Discuss the quality of the figure)
column12: Discuss the reality check (Was the reality check convincing? Was it clearly described?)
column13: Discuss the stability check (Was the check relevant to the finding(s)? Did it help to convince you that the finding corresponds to a real phenomenon?)

column1: code/run.sh, code/environment.yaml, report/lab1.tex, report/lab1.ipynb, or code/lab1.ipynb, code/clean.py, report/lab1.pdf
column2: Sufficient comments are included to understand code., Sufficient docstrings are included to understand APIs and functions., Consistent style is used.
column3: Yes. The code folder has run.sh that runs clean.py and models.py in order, so it is clear how the data and models are produced. The report is a pre-rendered PDF from LaTeX. The figures in the report come from the notebook or from the Python scripts, so in principle you could reproduce them by running the notebook and scripts. I did not run anything, but the structure is clear enough to reproduce the main results.
column4: Quite easy. There is a separate clean.py with one main function clean_data() that takes the raw dataframe and returns the cleaned one. The steps are described in the docstring and the report, so you can run it and get the same cleaning without changing their code.
column5: The report was clear and easy to read. The introduction states the goals and the sections follow a logical order from data and cleaning to EDA, findings, and models. I did not notice major grammar problems or places that were hard to follow.
column6: The cleaning is described in detail. The report lists seven steps: renaming columns, types, missing values, duplicates, range checks, consistency checks, and checking values against the codebook. The code matches this and explains inclusion and exclusion criteria. I did not see obvious inconsistencies that were missed; GCS and logical consistency are mentioned and the use of 91 and 92 is explained.
column7: The figures fit the report well. The age-stratified plot supports the discussion of different predictors by age. The outcome and EDA plots are referred to in the text. The stability and model figures are used where the report talks about bootstrap results and ROC. So the figures are relevant and discussed.
column11: The co-occurrence figure shows how often symptoms appear together in TBI patients. The layout and caption help you see that symptoms cluster. It supports the Bayesian and clustering discussion and is clear enough.
column12: The reality check is convincing. They compare cohort size and TBI rate to the original study and to general epidemiology, and they check that strong predictors match clinical knowledge. The small difference in sample size is explained. So the checks support that the data and outcome are plausible and well preprocessed.
column13: The stability check is relevant. They bootstrap the Bayes factors from Finding 3 and show that rare symptoms have wide intervals and common ones have narrow ones, and that the ranking of symptoms stays stable. So it supports that the finding is not just noise and that the main conclusion holds under sampling variation.

column1: code/run.sh, code/environment.yaml, report/lab1.tex, report/lab1.ipynb, or code/lab1.ipynb, code/clean.py, report/lab1.pdf
column2: Sufficient comments are included to understand code., Sufficient docstrings are included to understand APIs and functions., Consistent style is used.
column3: I checked the lab1.ipynb file, it gives a clear process of data cleaning and data preprocessing and how to generate the plot as shown in the report. It is easy to reproduce the result.
column4: The function is in the clean.py file. It is easy to use the clean functions.
column5: The report is good to read. I can easily follow its procedure, and I don't find grammar mistakes.
column6: The data cleaning/preprocessing are discussed under 2.2 & 2.3. The author lists some bullet points in data cleaning. But I think it is better to described the finding/problem in the dataset in a more detailed way.
column7: Yes, the figures and tables are reasonably included and discussed in the report.
column11: This is the interesting finding as it is in a bayesian view. The Bayes-factor analysis clearly distinguishes rare but highly informative symptoms from common but weak ones. The figure is very informative. Not sure if a table is allowed in the finding part.
column12: The reality check is strong and convincing. The author lists three kinds of external reference points and validate the dataset using these three sources.
column13: This is a strong stability check because it uses a reasonable bootstrap procedure, and shows that the main qualitative conclusion is robust to sampling variability. The figure effectively demonstrates that rare symptoms have larger Bayes factors but wider uncertainty intervals.
